item.description:
The First Blood of Christ begins the year in obedience, and His saving Name begins the covenant for the world.


On the eighth day, the Son of God submits to the Law He gave, receives the sign that binds Him to redeem us, and is publicly named Jesus — YHWH Saves.


In this sermon for January 1, Charles Wiese preaches Christ’s circumcision as:


the signature of perfect obedience,


the inauguration of substitutionary mercy,


the preview of the cross’s greater blood,


and the hinge between the old covenant’s sign and the new covenant’s gift: Baptism into Christ, not cutting — salvation by faith, not flesh.


A sermon rich in confessional theology, covenant fulfillment, the power of the Name of Jesus, and the comfort of God who keeps His promises by keeping His Son, and keeps you by His Spirit.


Start the year not with resolutions you must keep — but with the Savior who has kept all for you.

item.title: The God Who Tears Down Every Wall We Build-- Isaiah 58:1 – 59:3
item.itunes:title: The God Who Tears Down Every Wall We Build-- Isaiah 58:1 – 59:3
item.description:
Comfort for the terrified.

Warning for the pretender.

Salvation for the repentant.

For the mouth of the LORD has spoken.

And His Word does not return void.

Blow the trumpet. Share the Bread. Proclaim the Redeemer.

— Charles Wiese

item.title: Come to the Waters: The Word That Never Returns Empty
item.itunes:title: Come to the Waters: The Word That Never Returns Empty
item.description: A sermon on Isaiah 55:1–13, proclaimed in the Sword-of-God cadence. Christ stands at the center as the free feast of God for thirsty sinners. This message unfolds the everlasting covenant in David’s greater Son, the futility of self-purchased salvation, and the divine power of the Word that never returns empty but performs the Lord’s saving will. Preached for the pulpit, shaped by the Scriptures and the Lutheran Confessions, this sermon calls the poor, the wounded, and the grieving to the waters of Baptism, Absolution, and the Gospel’s unbreakable promise. The Word accomplishes its mission—faith comes by hearing—creation itself will one day sing at its triumph. Come, drink freely, and live.

item.title: When Truth Is Answered With Rage: The Anatomy of Opposition in Scripture and the Church
item.itunes:title: When Truth Is Answered With Rage: The Anatomy of Opposition in Scripture and the Church
item.description:
Why is truth so often met with shouting instead of reasoning, accusations instead of arguments, and violence instead of debate?


In this episode, Charles Wiese walks through Scripture and church history to expose a consistent pattern: those who oppose God’s truth frequently abandon reason and respond with insults, false charges, emotional outbursts, and force. From Pharaoh and the prophets, to Christ and the apostles, to the martyrs and Reformers, the same reaction appears again and again.

This episode also uncovers the deeper issue beneath the hostility—fear and insecurity. Those who appear most aggressive are often the most uncertain, and their rage reveals not confidence but terror that their beliefs cannot withstand examination.


A theologically rich, Scripture-saturated episode for Christians who are tired of being told that calm conviction is arrogance and who want to understand why truth provokes such hostility—and how to respond with faithfulness, clarity, and courage.

item.title: The King Who Bleeds in Exile--Matthew 2:13–18
item.itunes:title: The King Who Bleeds in Exile--Matthew 2:13–18
item.description: In this sermon, we explore the flight of Christ into Egypt, the wrath of Herod, and Rachel’s tears over Bethlehem. Discover how the newborn King—already threatened, already exiled—fulfills God’s promises, reigns by wounds, and brings ultimate comfort to a hurting world. Perfect for Christmas reflection, deepening faith, and understanding the cost of God’s salvation.

item.title: Follow Me — Not Another: Christ, the Cross, and the True Witness
item.itunes:title: Follow Me — Not Another: Christ, the Cross, and the True Witness
item.description: The risen Christ restores Peter, redirects comparison, and anchors discipleship in obedience to His personal call. In this doctrinally intense, Luther-style proclamation, we explore vocation without rivalry, martyrdom as hidden glory, eschatology without rumor, and the unshakable certainty of apostolic testimony. Christ does not call us into someone else’s path, but into faithful surrender to His — the Crucified and Living Lord who alone commands: Follow Me.

item.title: Stephen: The Open Heavens & the Standing Christ
item.itunes:title: Stephen: The Open Heavens & the Standing Christ
item.description: The first martyr’s blood is not tragedy—it is testimony. In this rhythmic, Luther-forged sermon, Deacon Stephen preaches Christ enthroned above Temple, Law, and self-preservation. The world stops its ears and lifts stones; heaven opens and Christ stands to receive His witness. Hear the clash of Law and Gospel, the courage of confession, and the prayer of forgiveness that planted the seed of an apostle. Delivered with a sworded cadence by Charles Wiese.

item.title: The Light the Darkness Could Not Comprehend — Christmas Day Sermon on John 1
item.itunes:title: The Light the Darkness Could Not Comprehend — Christmas Day Sermon on John 1
item.description:
The Gospel of Christmas roars before the manger: In the beginning was the Word.


This Christmas Day sermon proclaims Christ not as sentiment, but as Creator clothed in flesh, Light invading darkness, and Grace poured out for sinners.

Delivered in rhythmic cadence in the spirit of Luther, the sermon announces the eternal Word made visible, tangible, and given for you.


At the dining table, yet in the Divine Service—Christ comes where His Word is heard.


Here the mystery stands unshaken: the only-begotten Son, in the Father’s bosom, has declared God to us.

Hear the Light. Receive the Word. Depart in His peace.

item.title: Glory Hidden in Straw — A Christmas Sermon on Luke 2:1–14
item.itunes:title: Glory Hidden in Straw — A Christmas Sermon on Luke 2:1–14
item.description:
In this Christmas proclamation on Luke 2:1–14, the mystery of the Incarnation is preached in a rhythmic, Luther-style cadence meant for oral hearing and meditation.

Set against the decree of Caesar and the false glory of the world, this sermon proclaims Christ hidden in humility—laid in a manger, wrapped in cloths, announced not to kings but to shepherds. Here the theology of the cross is preached: God revealed where human reason least expects Him.

This sermon emphasizes:

The Incarnation and the hidden glory of God

Christ as Savior, Christ, and Lord

The overthrow of false gods and the preaching of the First Commandment

Justification by grace alone, for sinners

Peace on earth through the forgiveness of sins

item.title: “My Lord and My God” — Faith, Doubt, and the First Commandment (John 20:24–31)
item.itunes:title: “My Lord and My God” — Faith, Doubt, and the First Commandment (John 20:24–31)
item.description:
What is faith—and what makes something a god?

In this sermon on John 20:24–31, the risen Christ meets Thomas not with rebuke, but with peace, wounds, and a promise. From Thomas’s demand for sight to his great confession—“My Lord and my God”—we learn that faith does not rest on proof, feelings, or certainty, but on Christ crucified and risen for sinners.

Drawing deeply from Luther’s teaching on the First Commandment, this sermon exposes the hidden idols of sight, reason, and control, and proclaims the Gospel:

God has bound Himself to His Word, His wounds, and His promise of life.

“These things are written so that you may believe… and have life in His name.”

A confessional Lutheran sermon for doubters, catechumens, and weary saints alike.

item.title: Revelation: A Sword of God Reading
item.itunes:title: Revelation: A Sword of God Reading
item.description: Experience the prophetic power of God’s Word with a full reading of the Book of Revelation, translated in the Sword of God style. Perfect for meditation, reflection, and deepening your understanding of Scripture. Narrated with care and reverence, this recording brings the visions, warnings, and promises of Revelation vividly to life.

item.title: Philemon — A Reading from the God’s Word Translation (GW)
item.itunes:title: Philemon — A Reading from the God’s Word Translation (GW)
item.description:
This episode features a complete reading

of the Epistle to Philemon

from the God’s Word® Translation (GW) of the Holy Scriptures.


Philemon is Paul’s personal appeal on behalf of Onesimus,

where Christian freedom, authority, forgiveness,

and love in Christ are brought together in a single letter.


This reading is presented without commentary,

for hearing and meditation.


Scripture quotations are taken from the God’s Word® Translation,

copyright © 1995, 2003, 2013

God’s Word to the Nations.

Used by permission.

item.title: Philemon — A Reading from the Sword of God Translation
item.itunes:title: Philemon — A Reading from the Sword of God Translation
item.description:
This episode features a complete reading of the Epistle to Philemon

from The Sword of God translation of the Holy Scriptures.


Philemon is the shortest of Paul’s letters, yet among the most demanding.

Here the apostle does not command, but appeals — binding conscience, love,

and Christian freedom together in Christ.


This reading is presented without commentary,

for proclamation and hearing.

item.title: Galatians — Read Aloud from The Sword of God Translation
item.itunes:title: Galatians — Read Aloud from The Sword of God Translation
item.description:
This episode features a continuous reading of the Epistle to the Galatians from The Sword of God translation.


There is no commentary and no discussion—only the Word of God read aloud as it was given to be heard. Galatians confronts false gospels, exposes enslaving religion, and proclaims justification by faith in Christ alone with apostolic urgency and clarity.


Listen without rushing. Let the Law speak where it accuses, and let the Gospel speak where it gives life.

item.title: The Sword of God: Introducing the Translation — Galatians and Beyond
item.itunes:title: The Sword of God: Introducing the Translation — Galatians and Beyond
item.description: Discover The Sword of God, a bold, faithful English translation designed to be heard as Scripture was meant to be—alive, sharp, and uncompromising. In this episode, we introduce the translation’s method, textual basis, and guiding convictions, and explain how upcoming readings of the Epistle to the Galatians will allow God’s Word to speak directly to your heart. Perfect for daily listening, study, and reflection.

item.title: Gaudete Sunday: Faithful Stewards, Repentant Hearts (1 Corinthians 4:1–5; Matthew 11:2–10)
item.itunes:title: Gaudete Sunday: Faithful Stewards, Repentant Hearts (1 Corinthians 4:1–5; Matthew 11:2–10)
item.description:
On Gaudete Sunday the Church is called to rejoice—but not with shallow cheerfulness. True Christian joy is grounded in repentance, faith, and fidelity to the Word of God.


In this sermon on 1 Corinthians 4:1–5 and Matthew 11:2–10, we hear St. Paul’s call to be faithful stewards of God’s mysteries and Jesus’ testimony about John the Baptist, the preacher of repentance who prepared the way for the Lord. Even in doubt, suffering, and imprisonment, John clung to the promises of Christ.


This sermon calls hearers to repent of false expectations of God, to submit to His Word rather than human judgment, and to rejoice because the Lord is near. It also serves as an introduction to catechetical instruction, showing why the Church must clearly teach what she believes, confesses, and practices.


Gaudete—Rejoice. The Judge who is coming is also your Savior.

item.title: Can Someone Teach False Doctrine and Not Be a False Teacher?
item.itunes:title: Can Someone Teach False Doctrine and Not Be a False Teacher?
item.description: Can someone teach false doctrine and still not be a false teacher? Many Christians instinctively shift the conversation to defending a person’s salvation whenever false teaching is named—but Scripture doesn’t. In this episode, we confront the biblical reality that false teaching produces false teachers, that the Church is commanded to judge all doctrine, and that refusing to do so is not humility but disobedience. We also examine the often-overlooked truth that some in Scripture spoke rightly and taught truly, yet were not saved. This is a necessary conversation about guarding Christ’s truth without presuming final judgment over souls—recovering a clarity the modern church has largely lost.

item.title: They Did Not Understand—Because They Did Not Believe: When Clarity Offends the Heart
item.itunes:title: They Did Not Understand—Because They Did Not Believe: When Clarity Offends the Heart
item.description:
Jesus did not speak in riddles when He told His disciples about His death and resurrection — Scripture says He spoke plainly. And yet they did not understand, not because His words were unclear, but because they could not believe He truly meant what He said.


In this episode, we trace a consistent pattern in the Gospels: whenever Christ speaks with clarity, unbelief disguises itself as confusion. From the disciples’ resistance to a crucified Messiah to modern claims that the Bible is “unclear” about the sacraments, chastity, judgment, care for the poor, and the treatment of outsiders, the issue remains the same. The problem is not ambiguity in Scripture, but hearts unwilling to accept what Jesus plainly teaches.


This is a theological and pastoral examination of how disbelief manufactures uncertainty — and why faith begins not with reinterpretation, but with trusting that Christ means exactly what He says. ✝️

item.title: Luther's Preface to the Large Catechism
item.itunes:title: Luther's Preface to the Large Catechism
item.description:
“This work is written for those who wish to be Christians.”


In this episode, we present a straight, uninterrupted reading of Martin Luther’s Preface to the Large Catechism. Luther speaks plainly and forcefully about the crisis of Christian instruction, the neglect of the Catechism, and the grave responsibility placed especially on pastors, preachers, fathers, and heads of households.


This is not commentary or paraphrase, but Luther’s own words—calling Christians back to daily repentance, disciplined teaching, and reverent engagement with God’s Word. Written for clergy and laity alike, the Preface sets the tone for the Large Catechism as a pastoral and evangelical work, aimed at forming faithful Christians rather than producing mere outward conformity.


Listen prayerfully, with the seriousness Luther intended, as he exhorts Christ’s Church to take doctrine, vocation, and catechesis seriously—for the sake of souls and the glory of God.

item.title: Populus Zion — “Lift Up Your Heads” (Luke 21:25–36)
item.itunes:title: Populus Zion — “Lift Up Your Heads” (Luke 21:25–36)
item.description:
On this Second Sunday in Advent, Jesus directs His Church to watchfulness, prayer, and confidence as we await His return. In Luke 21:25–36, Christ speaks of signs in heaven and earth that will cause fear among the nations, yet He calls believers to stand tall and lift up their heads, for their redemption is drawing near.


This sermon explores the difference between fear and faith, the purpose of the signs of the end, and Christ’s warning against spiritual sleep brought on by dissipation, drunkenness, and the cares of this life. Above all, we hear the enduring comfort of Jesus’ promise: “Heaven and earth will pass away, but My words will never pass away.”


A message of Advent hope, repentance, and steady confidence in Christ’s unchanging Word.

item.title: Is the Organ the Gold Standard? Rethinking Instruments in Christian Worship
item.itunes:title: Is the Organ the Gold Standard? Rethinking Instruments in Christian Worship
item.description:
For many Christians—especially within liturgical traditions—the pipe organ is treated as the unquestioned benchmark of serious, reverent worship. But is this conviction truly grounded in Scripture, the Reformation, or the theology of Martin Luther? Or has a historical preference quietly become a theological assumption?


In this episode, we challenge the idea that any single instrument deserves “gold standard” status in Christian worship and ask a more Lutheran question: Do our instruments serve the Word of God and congregational singing—or do they replace them?


We examine the strengths and dangers of a wide range of instruments, including:


Pipe organs


Lutes and guitars


Banjos, mandolins, and fiddles


Percussion and rhythm


Horns and brass



Drawing from Luther’s musical theology, the Psalms, and the Reformation’s emphasis on the living, sung Word, this episode critiques performance-driven worship and calls the Church back to music that forms, teaches, and proclaims Christ through the active voice of the congregation.


This is not a debate over traditional versus contemporary styles.

It is a theological examination of Word versus spectacle, participation versus passivity, and worship shaped by Christ rather than custom.


🎶 The gold standard of Christian worship is not an instrument—it is the Word of Christ clearly sung by His people.

item.title: Demon Possession: Luther, Walther, and the War Modern Lutheranism Forgot
item.itunes:title: Demon Possession: Luther, Walther, and the War Modern Lutheranism Forgot
item.description:
Modern Lutheranism often speaks freely about psychology, trauma, and brokenness—but goes strangely silent when Scripture speaks plainly about demons. In this episode, we recover the bold, biblical realism of Martin Luther and C. F. W. Walther, who taught without embarrassment that the devil is real, active, and defeated only by Christ Himself.


Drawing from Scripture, Luther’s writings, and Walther’s theology, this podcast exposes how contemporary Lutheran teaching has demythologized spiritual warfare, replacing repentance and divine deliverance with therapeutic language and pastoral silence. Luther saw false doctrine itself as demonic work; Walther warned that when pastors refuse to warn, wolves are emboldened.


This is not sensationalism. It is confession.


Christ still casts out demons by His Word. Baptism is still a violent rescue from Satan’s kingdom. Absolution still releases captives. The Church does not need new methods—she needs renewed faith in the weapons Christ has already given.


“For this purpose the Son of God was manifested, that He might destroy the works of the devil.” (1 John 3:8)

item.title: When Tolerated Sin Becomes Mortal Sin
item.itunes:title: When Tolerated Sin Becomes Mortal Sin
item.description:
When Tolerated Sin Becomes Mortal Sin is a bold, confessional episode that confronts one of the most dangerous lies in modern Christianity: the idea that sins can be managed, excused, or tolerated without destroying faith. Drawing deeply from Holy Scripture and the Lutheran Confessions, this episode explains why any sin that is defended or left unrepented becomes a mortal sin—not because it is unusually shocking, but because it extinguishes faith by rejecting repentance.


This episode also addresses the grave responsibility placed upon pastors. According to God’s Word and the Confessions, shepherds who refuse to warn their people—even about common or socially acceptable sins—become sharers in those sins through their silence. Covering passages from Ezekiel, the Epistles, and the Augsburg Confession, this podcast exposes false mercy, sentimental love, and pastoral cowardice, while calling the Church back to true repentance and true Gospel comfort.


This is not an episode about shame—but about salvation. Where sin is named, Christ is proclaimed. Where repentance is preached, forgiveness is real. Where the Law does its killing work, the Gospel raises the dead.

item.title: Luther on the Ministry: The Divine Call, Human Ordination, and the Crisis of Romanizing Lutheranism
item.itunes:title: Luther on the Ministry: The Divine Call, Human Ordination, and the Crisis of Romanizing Lutheranism
item.description:
What makes a pastor a pastor? Is it ordination—or the call of the Church through Christ’s Word?


In this episode, we examine Martin Luther’s doctrine of the holy ministry through his Letter to the Bohemians and the Smalcald Articles, showing that ordination is a human церemony that confirms the call, not the source of ministerial authority. Luther’s own words demonstrate that where the orthodox episcopacy fails or refuses to serve the Gospel, the Church itself retains the right and duty to call ministers so that it may not be left without the Word of Christ.


We then confront the danger identified by C. F. W. Walther and Francis Pieper—what they called Romanizing Lutheranism—the attempt to ground ministerial authority in ordination or office rather than in Christ’s Word and the divine call. Drawing also from the LCMS Brief Statement, this episode applies Luther’s theology to times of doctrinal collapse, when faithful congregations may be forced to act outside normal structures for the sake of the Gospel.


This is not an argument for disorder or individualism, but a confession that Christ rules His Church through His Word, and that the ministry exists to serve that Word—never to replace it.

item.title: The Day That Burns and the Dawn That Heals — Malachi 4:1–6 (Populus Zion)
item.itunes:title: The Day That Burns and the Dawn That Heals — Malachi 4:1–6 (Populus Zion)
item.description:
Malachi’s final prophecy strikes with the force of a divine hammer. The Day of the Lord is coming—burning like a furnace for the arrogant and unrepentant, yet rising like a healing dawn for those who fear His Name. In this sermon for Populus Zion, we confront the reality of God’s judgment, the seriousness of sin, the necessity of repentance, and the comfort of Christ’s saving righteousness. The same Day that turns the wicked to ash lifts the faithful like calves released from their stalls.

Lift up your heads, People of Zion—your King is coming with fire and with healing.

item.title: Every Christian a Theologian: Why “Leave Doctrine to the Experts” Is a Lie
item.itunes:title: Every Christian a Theologian: Why “Leave Doctrine to the Experts” Is a Lie
item.description: This episode dismantles the modern myth that theology is for experts. We walk through Scripture, the Reformation, and church history to show that every Christian is called to be a theologian—and that you cannot judge truth without knowing God’s Word yourself. We look at biblical examples of experts who misled the people, the Bereans who tested Paul, and the dangers of turning theology into an academic exercise that tames God and silences Scripture. A call for Christians to take up their Bibles and reclaim the faith delivered to the saints.

item.title: Ordained by God: Why Romans 13 Applies to America—Elected or Not
item.itunes:title: Ordained by God: Why Romans 13 Applies to America—Elected or Not
item.description:
Many claim Romans 13 doesn’t apply to Americans because we elect our leaders and don’t have a king. But this episode shows from Scripture and history that authority comes from God alone—not from voters, not from “consent of the governed,” and not from Enlightenment philosophy.


We’ll explore:

• How Rome had elections and mixed government when Paul wrote Romans 13

• Why “government by consent” contradicts the Bible

• How people don’t truly behave as though they consent

• How God identifies rulers by the authority He gives, not by human approval

• How God uses even tyrants to maintain order

• And how God’s warning in 1 Samuel 8 about kings perfectly describes American presidents


A theologically rich, Scripture-driven exploration of authority, vocation, and God’s ordering of civil life.

item.title: The Hireling Flees: When Pastors Refuse to Confront Sin
item.itunes:title: The Hireling Flees: When Pastors Refuse to Confront Sin
item.description:
In this episode, we confront one of the most dangerous crises in the Church today: pastors who behave like hirelings rather than shepherds. Christ warns that the hireling sees the wolf coming and flees—leaving the sheep to be devoured. This sermon exposes the deadly reality that any sin excused, minimized, or explained away becomes a mortal sin, not because God is unwilling to forgive, but because defended sin rejects His forgiveness.


We explore why faithful pastors must rebuke, discipline, and speak with the authority of God’s Word—even when the sins in question are popular, socially accepted, or deeply rooted in congregational life. Silence does not save. Silence makes shepherds partakers in the sins of their flock.


This episode is a call to courage for pastors, a call to repentance for congregations, and a reminder that Christ the Good Shepherd lays down His life for sinners—not to leave them in their sin, but to deliver them from it.

item.title: Little Ones Belong to Me: The Salvation of Infants, the Stillborn, and the Miscarried
item.itunes:title: Little Ones Belong to Me: The Salvation of Infants, the Stillborn, and the Miscarried
item.description:
In this theologically rich and deeply pastoral episode, we explore the sure and certain comfort God gives concerning the salvation of infants — including those who die before birth through miscarriage or stillbirth. Drawing from Scripture and the writings of Martin Luther, we show how God always saves through His Word, how faith is always His gift, and why the so-called “age of accountability” undermines justification by faith alone.


We look at the biblical truth that infants can and do believe, even from the womb, and how the inability to articulate faith does not mean faith is absent. We also address the tender question of the unbaptized miscarried or stillborn child, showing how God’s mercy and His powerful Word reach even into the womb.


This episode is for grieving parents, pastors, and all Christians seeking Christ’s comfort and clarity:

He gathers the little ones to Himself, and of such is the Kingdom of God.

item.title: The Fountain of Living Waters — Jeremiah 2:13
item.itunes:title: The Fountain of Living Waters — Jeremiah 2:13
item.description:
Jeremiah 2:13 is one of the sharpest diagnoses of the human heart in all of Scripture. God says His people committed two evils: they forsook Him, the fountain of living waters, and they built broken cisterns that cannot hold water.


In this episode, we explore what it means to seek God as the only true source of life—and how every age, including ours, tries to replace Him with empty substitutes. From political idolatry to self-made spirituality, from material security to personal righteousness, we trace how modern people still carve out broken cisterns and remain spiritually thirsty.


Drawing on Scripture, Luther, and the Lutheran Confessions, this sermon calls us to repent of false trust and to drink deeply from Christ, the true and living fountain. The gospel is not that we claw our way back to God—but that the Fountain Himself comes to us with grace, forgiveness, and life.


If you’re thirsty, exhausted, disappointed in the world, or tired of empty wells—this episode is for you. Come and drink.

item.title: Speaking with Authority: Recovering the Voice of Scripture
item.itunes:title: Speaking with Authority: Recovering the Voice of Scripture
item.description:
this episode, we explore why Jesus “spoke as one who had authority, and not as the scribes,” and why Christians today are called to speak with that same Scriptural confidence. We look at how the Apostles, the Prophets, the Lutheran Confessions, Martin Luther, and C. F. W. Walther all understood that God’s Word must be spoken clearly, boldly, and without hesitation.


We expose the false humility that hides behind doubt, the modern tendency to speak more confidently about politics and science than about Christ, and the pressure to soften or hedge God’s Word. And we show how Scripture not only gives us the doctrines of the faith—it gives us its language and tone, and commands us to imitate it.


This episode is a call to let God’s Word reshape our speech so we may confess with clarity: “Thus says the Lord.”

item.title: Against Theodicy: Why God Does Not Need Defending
item.itunes:title: Against Theodicy: Why God Does Not Need Defending
item.description:
In this episode, we dive into one of the most difficult and misunderstood topics in Christianity: theodicy—the human attempt to justify God’s actions and explain why He allows suffering. Using Scripture, Luther, and the Lutheran Confessions, this episode argues that the Bible rejects every form of theodicy. God does not ask to be defended. He reveals Himself where He chooses: in Christ crucified.


We explore Job’s debates, Paul’s forceful warnings in Romans 9, Isaiah’s proclamation of God’s hiddenness, Jesus’ refusal to provide explanations, Luther’s theology of the hidden and revealed God, and the Confessions’ rejection of speculation.


The Christian answer to suffering is not philosophical. It is personal: Jesus Christ crucified for sinners.

Silence before the hidden God.

Faith in the revealed God.

The cross—not speculation—is our comfort.

item.title: Luther and Leviathan
item.itunes:title: Luther and Leviathan
item.description:
In this episode, we explore one of the most mysterious figures in Scripture—Leviathan—and how Martin Luther understood it. By walking through the book of Job, the Psalms, Isaiah, and Luther’s reflections, we see Leviathan not as a puzzle to decode but as a witness to God’s sovereignty, freedom, and hidden majesty.


Luther insisted that Leviathan confronts us with a God who cannot be managed, predicted, or tamed. The point is not to identify Leviathan but to be humbled by the God who commands what we cannot comprehend. When God answers Job out of the whirlwind, He does not offer explanations—He reveals His overwhelming lordship over all creation, including its most terrifying mysteries.


This episode unpacks:


• What Scripture reveals about Leviathan

• How Luther interprets this creature in Job and the Psalms

• Leviathan as a rebuke to human pride, speculation, and rationalism

• How God uses Leviathan to silence theodicies

• Why the hidden God terrifies—but the revealed God comforts


Join us as we explore how Leviathan exposes the limits of human understanding and drives us to trust not in answers but in the God who has revealed Himself in Christ.

item.title: The God Who Seeks to Kill His Prophet So He May Truly Live
item.itunes:title: The God Who Seeks to Kill His Prophet So He May Truly Live
item.description:
In this episode, we explore one of the most startling passages in the Old Testament: the moment in Exodus 4:24–26 where “the LORD met Moses and sought to kill him.” Why would God seemingly ambush His own chosen prophet? What covenant truth had Moses ignored? And how does this terrifying encounter actually reveal the gospel, the mercy of God, and the blood that saves sinners?

Drawing deeply on Martin Luther’s theology of law and gospel, divine chastening, vocation, and the covenant, we uncover how this strange event prefigures Christ and how God still deals with His people today. A powerful look at the God who wounds in order to heal and kills in order to make alive.

item.title: When the Graves Broke Open: Luther on the Saints Raised at Christ’s Death
item.itunes:title: When the Graves Broke Open: Luther on the Saints Raised at Christ’s Death
item.description:
In this episode, we explore one of the strangest and most awe-inspiring moments in the Gospels—Matthew 27:52–53, where the graves open and many saints rise from the dead at the moment of Christ’s crucifixion. What actually happened? Why does Matthew include it? And how did Martin Luther understand this extraordinary event?

Drawing from Luther’s Postils, Easter sermons, and his teaching on Christ’s victory over death, we unpack this passage as the cosmic proclamation that the kingdom of death has fallen. These risen saints are not myth, metaphor, or mystery—they are the first visible shockwave of the new creation, the ringing bells of Easter Sunday, the first trophies of Christ’s triumph. A theologically rich, pastoral, and Christ-centered journey into one of Scripture’s most neglected texts.

item.title: Fallen Sons, Mighty Men: Luther on the Nephilim
item.itunes:title: Fallen Sons, Mighty Men: Luther on the Nephilim
item.description:
Step into one of the strangest chapters of Scripture—Genesis 6. In this episode, we cut through modern myths and sensationalist nonsense to explain the Nephilim through the clear, sober lens of Dr. Martin Luther. Who were the “sons of God”? Why did the Church nearly disappear before the Flood? Why does this short passage provoke so much confusion?

Using Luther’s Genesis lectures, we explore how the line of Seth fell into apostasy, how the Nephilim became the violent “mighty men” of a corrupt age, and how God preserved the promise of Christ through Noah. This is not an episode about angels breeding hybrids—it’s about what happens when believers marry unbelievers, when the Church embraces the world, and when faith is despised. A doctrinal, pastoral, and Christ-centered look at one of the most misunderstood texts in the Bible.

item.title: From Greek to Gridirons: How Christian Colleges Lost Their Souls
item.itunes:title: From Greek to Gridirons: How Christian Colleges Lost Their Souls
item.description:
Most Christian colleges in America began as humble training schools for pastors—places centered on Scripture, classical languages, and the formation of faithful ministers. But over the decades, many drifted into generic liberal arts institutions, dropped Greek and Hebrew, hired secular faculty, and embraced athletics-driven identity.


This episode traces how it happened, why it keeps happening, and what it reveals about mission drift in the Church. From accreditation pressures to the cult of football, we’ll examine the subtle compromises that reshaped Christian higher education—and what true faithfulness would look like today.


If you’ve ever wondered why so many Christian colleges no longer resemble the schools their founders envisioned, this episode will give you the story behind the story.

item.title: The Wheels Full of Eyes: The God Whose Glory Moves
item.itunes:title: The Wheels Full of Eyes: The God Whose Glory Moves
item.description:
In this deep dive into Ezekiel 1, we explore one of the most mysterious and awe-inspiring visions in all of Scripture—the wheels within wheels, the living creatures, the fire, the lightning, the throne, and the Man who sits upon it. What do these wheels full of eyes mean? Why does God reveal Himself this way to a broken, exiled people? And what does this vision teach us about Christ, His holiness, His Gospel, and His presence in our lives today?


Join us as we uncover:


The mobility of God’s glory in exile and suffering


How God’s all-seeing eyes bring both judgment and comfort


Why God reveals His terrifying holiness before He speaks mercy


How the rainbow around the throne proclaims the Gospel


How this entire vision points directly to Christ, the true Glory of God


What it means for the Church today to follow the God who moves



This episode is a journey into the fire and the thunder—a call to repentance, awe, and comfort in the God who sees, moves, and saves.

item.title: Stop Believing in Yourself
item.itunes:title: Stop Believing in Yourself
item.description:
Modern America tells us to “follow your dreams,” “trust your heart,” and “believe in yourself.” But Scripture teaches the exact opposite: do not trust in yourself at all. True humility is not personality style or emotional softness—it is absolute dependence on Christ alone.


In this episode, we examine the Bible’s theology of humility and its conflict with both pagan virtue and American values. We explore how the cross destroys our self-confidence, how suffering teaches humility, and how only Christ gives us His humility through Word and Sacrament.


Featuring direct quotations from Martin Luther, this episode confronts the myths of the “self-made man,” exposes the idolatry of personal greatness, and proclaims the good news: you don’t have to believe in yourself—Christ is enough.


> “We are beggars. This is true.” —Martin Luther

item.title: Thanksgiving in Sackcloth: Humiliation Before Feasting
item.itunes:title: Thanksgiving in Sackcloth: Humiliation Before Feasting
item.description:
Modern Thanksgiving has forgotten its first course: repentance. In this sermon patterned after C. F. W. Walther’s preaching, we examine Joel 2:12–14 and the historic Lutheran practice of beginning Thanksgiving with a Day of Humiliation, fasting, and confession of sins. True thanksgiving does not begin with a feast, but with a broken and contrite heart. Before we pick up the fork, we kneel before the cross.


This episode exposes the sins concealed behind abundance, deflates our pride in the works of our own hands, and teaches how Christians feast as forgiven sinners, not boastful consumers. From our tables to our hearts, we learn that thanksgiving is not earned by labor, but given only through God’s mercy in Christ.


If our thanksgiving is not rooted in repentance, it is not thanksgiving at all.

Let us humble ourselves before we celebrate.

item.title: Faith That Cannot Be Shaken: Daniel in the Lion’s Den
item.itunes:title: Faith That Cannot Be Shaken: Daniel in the Lion’s Den
item.description: Faith that cannot be shaken: Daniel in the lion’s den. In this sermon, we explore Daniel 6:1–28 and discover how God’s faithful servants endure trials, stand firm in prayer, and witness God’s glory even under threat. Learn how Daniel’s courage, prayer, and integrity point us to Christ and inspire us to live boldly in a world that resists God. Tune in for practical lessons on enduring trials, trusting God over circumstances, and living a faith that shines for His glory.

item.title: Wake Up and See Your King! — Advent 1 (One-Year Lectionary)
item.itunes:title: Wake Up and See Your King! — Advent 1 (One-Year Lectionary)
item.description:
The Church year doesn’t begin with sentimental holiday cheer or soft Christmas nostalgia. It begins with a trumpet blast: “Wake up! Your King is coming!” In this Advent 1 sermon on Jeremiah 23:5–8, Romans 13:11–14, and Matthew 21:1–9, we confront the comfort-driven sleep of modern Christianity and the false hopes we place in politics, culture, and personal identity. We meet a King unlike the world’s kings—One who rides in on a donkey, hides Himself in humble means, and comes not to inspire us, but to save us from our sin.


Learn why Advent is not a countdown to Christmas but a call to repentance, a comfort for the weary, and the promise that our humble King will return in glory.

Repent. Rejoice. Hosanna to the Son of David!


📖 Texts: Jeremiah 23:5–8; Romans 13:11–14; Matthew 21:1–9

item.title: Luther’s “On the Councils and the Church” — How Christ Builds His People
item.itunes:title: Luther’s “On the Councils and the Church” — How Christ Builds His People
item.description:
Where is the true Church?

If councils fail, if popes contradict themselves, if pastors fall into sin, and if whole institutions collapse — how can Christians recognize where Christ’s people really are?


In this episode we explore Martin Luther’s nearly forgotten masterpiece, “On the Councils and the Church” (1539). Written near the end of his life, Luther asks a question that is just as urgent today as it was during the Reformation:


> The Church is not defined by power or popularity, but by Christ’s presence.




Luther answers by giving seven biblical marks of the Church—rooted in the Gospel, not human systems:


The pure preaching of the Word


Baptism


The Lord’s Supper


The Office of the Keys


Worship and the Catechism


Suffering and the Cross


Love for the weak



This episode reveals how Luther both honored the early councils and rejected the idolatry of human authority. Most importantly, we find comfort in the promise that Christ Himself builds His Church and will never abandon it.


✝ The Church is not ours to preserve. It is ours to receive.

item.title: Peacemakers, Patriots, and the Sword: How Christians Lost Their Theology of War
item.itunes:title: Peacemakers, Patriots, and the Sword: How Christians Lost Their Theology of War
item.description:
Christians once refused to kill. The early Church, including the Council of Nicaea, demanded that soldiers lay down their swords after baptism. Augustine allowed war only as a tragic act of love. Luther insisted that Christians may fight only in truly just wars—and only according to conscience, not blind obedience to the state.


So how did we get from “Blessed are the peacemakers” to treating military service as a Christian duty for every citizen?


In this episode, we explore:


Why early Christians forbade military service


How Luther’s two kingdoms prohibit blind obedience to government


How modern patriotism rewrote Romans 13


Why the Bible celebrates peacemakers, not national warriors



This isn’t a call to pacifism—it’s a call to repentance. Before we salute soldiers, we must bow to Christ, the Prince of Peace, who conquered not by killing enemies but by dying for them.

item.title: Touched Not: David, Tyrants, and God’s Anointed
item.itunes:title: Touched Not: David, Tyrants, and God’s Anointed
item.description:
What do you do when the leader God put over you becomes a tyrant? What if he even tries to kill you? In this episode, we explore why David refused to kill Saul—even when he had every political and personal reason to. David knew Saul’s corruption, but he also knew something deeper: the kingdom of God does not advance by revolt, revenge, or the sword.


We dive deep into:


Why David refused to “touch the Lord’s anointed”


How resisting a ruler differs from overthrowing one


Why God, not man, removes tyrants


What Christians can do under unjust governments


How David’s suffering mirrors the kingship of Christ



Revolution is easy. Faithful patience is hard.

David shows us the way of Christ: the kingdom comes by promise and suffering, not by seizing power.


> “The LORD judge between me and you.” — 1 Samuel 24:12

item.title: Christ in the Psalms: Praying in His Wounds
item.itunes:title: Christ in the Psalms: Praying in His Wounds
item.description:
The Psalms are more than ancient poetry—they are the voice of Christ Himself. In this episode we uncover how every Psalm finds its fulfillment in Jesus, and how His prayers become ours through baptism, suffering, repentance, joy, and spiritual warfare.


We explore how:


Jesus is the true Singer of the Psalms


We learn to pray because Christ prays for us


Psalms shape our emotions, our endurance, and our faith


Psalms of lament, praise, confession, and victory become our own stories


Our suffering is carried inside the prayers of Christ Himself



We walk through key Psalms—22, 23, 51, and 2—to see how Christ lives, dies, rises, and reigns in their verses. When we pray the Psalms, we are praying inside His wounds, His victory, and His everlasting kingship.


🎙 Come discover how the Psalms are the prayer book of Jesus… and the living voice of His Church.

item.title: Against the Conspiracy
item.itunes:title: Against the Conspiracy
item.description:
Against the Conspiracy explores the biblical command not to listen to conspiracy theories and how they distract Christians from their true spiritual enemies. Many believe they are practicing “discernment” by hunting for hidden plots, but Scripture forbids this false wisdom:


> “Do not call conspiracy all that this people calls conspiracy, nor fear what they fear.” — Isaiah 8:12




This episode shows how conspiracy fear replaces the fear of God, how it moves the church to fight flesh and blood instead of principalities and powers (Ephesians 6:12), and how blaming human enemies can even recruit us into the camp of Gog and Magog, the final raging of the nations against Christ (Revelation 20:7–8).


Instead of panic, accusation, and worldly warfare, Christ calls His church to repentance, worship, prayer, love, and trust in His sovereign rule over every ruler on earth (Romans 13:1, Daniel 2:21).


The danger is not simply believing a wrong theory. The danger is believing that men control history instead of God.


Listen to learn how the saints wage the true war—not with fear, but with faith in the crucified and risen King who reigns over all.

item.title: How Debate Starves the Soul
item.itunes:title: How Debate Starves the Soul
item.description:
In a world addicted to arguing—especially on social media—Jesus gives us a command that many Christians regularly break: “Do not give dogs what is holy; do not throw your pearls before pigs.” (Matthew 7:6)


This episode explores why continuing to debate fools, mockers, and hardened hearts is not a sign of faithfulness, but a temptation toward self-justification and spiritual immaturity. We’ll look at:


Why Jesus sometimes refused to speak


How arguing corrodes humility and faith


Why “winning” an online debate may actually be a sin


How silence can be a confession of trust in Christ


The difference between evangelism and ego


Why spiritually mature Christians know when to stop talking



The Gospel is treasure. It is not entertainment. And Christ does not ask you to win arguments—He asks you to trust the Word and obey Him even when that means walking away in silence.


Sometimes the holiest thing you can say… is nothing.

item.title: Against Apologetics
item.itunes:title: Against Apologetics
item.description:
Is Christianity really on trial? Modern churches act as if God needs evidence presented, arguments defended, or scientific data compiled to make Him believable. But historic Lutheran theology says something radically different: the Gospel does not ask for proof, and the Holy Spirit never converts through arguments.


In this episode, we explore why Luther, Walther, and Pieper rejected modern apologetics, not because Christianity is weak, but because reason is fallen and cannot produce saving faith. We’ll see how:


Faith is not an intellectual conclusion.


Reason is not spiritually neutral.


Evidence cannot raise the spiritually dead.


The Word itself creates faith without our defense.



Instead of defending God, we proclaim Him. Christ does the defending.

The Gospel needs preachers, not lawyers.


📌 “Faith comes by hearing… not arguing.”

—Romans 10:17

item.title: Why Luther Was Too Biblical for Monarchists and Too Christian for Democrats
item.itunes:title: Why Luther Was Too Biblical for Monarchists and Too Christian for Democrats
item.description:
> Government is not a contract of the people, nor a divine privilege of kings, but a calling instituted by God.

Yet no earthly office ever gains authority over the conscience. That belongs to Christ alone.




In this episode, titled “Why Luther Was Too Biblical for Monarchists and Too Christian for Democrats,” we dig deeply into the biblical and Lutheran doctrine of civil authority, showing how it differs sharply from:


The Divine Right of Kings


Modern “government by consent”


and churchly coercion over conscience



Drawing from Scripture, the Book of Concord, and the writings of Luther, C.F.W. Walther, and Franz Pieper, we explore:


📌 Why Christians honor rulers—even wicked ones

📌 Why Christians must never obey sinful commands

📌 Why both monarchies and democracies become tyrannical when they demand the soul

📌 Why the conscience is God’s throne—and never Caesar’s


This episode challenges the idols of both the ancient throne and the modern crowd, showing that the Christian citizen is loyal, peaceful, bold, and unyielding when conscience is at stake.


> “He who surrenders his conscience surrenders the kingdom of God itself.” —C.F.W. Walther

item.title: The Christian Gentleman’s Wardrobe
item.itunes:title: The Christian Gentleman’s Wardrobe
item.description:
This week we’re talking about a topic most men overlook, yet it shapes how they carry themselves in work, family life, leadership, and faith: how a Christian man dresses. We’ll explore what Scripture reveals about the way men present themselves, why appearance mattered from Adam onward, and what the Bible says a believer should actually put on every day.


We’ll talk about confidence, dignity, discipline, and how being properly prepared each morning affects marriage, fatherhood, career, and spiritual battles. We’ll draw from the Old and New Testaments, and even get insight from Luther and Walther on what makes a man’s appearance honorable.

item.title: Luther, Textual Variants, and the Autograph Debate: Can We Trust the Bible We Have?
item.itunes:title: Luther, Textual Variants, and the Autograph Debate: Can We Trust the Bible We Have?
item.description:
For many Christians today, defending the Bible means defending the idea of “perfect original manuscripts” — autographs no one has ever seen and that no longer exist. But Martin Luther never argued this way. He didn’t build faith on lost documents. He trusted the Scriptures that were actually in the hands and ears of the Church.


In this episode, we explore:


how Luther defended Scripture without appealing to autographs


how the Holy Spirit gives authority to the Bible in the present


why Luther welcomed textual variants rather than fearing them


how Franz Pieper and later Lutheran teachers shifted the conversation


how seminaries changed in the 20th century, and what we’ve lost


how the Church today can return to Luther’s confidence in the Scriptures we actually have and hear



This episode challenges both fundamentalism and modern skepticism by returning to a distinctly Lutheran understanding of Scripture — one that rests not on perfect copies, but on the perfect Christ who speaks through His Word today.


📖 “The Holy Spirit is no skeptic.” — Luther

item.title: Humiliation & Thanksgiving: When Fasting Preceded Feasting
item.itunes:title: Humiliation & Thanksgiving: When Fasting Preceded Feasting
item.description:
Before America ever set a table for Thanksgiving, Christians across the colonies gathered for something very different: a public day of fasting, humiliation, and repentance. Why did our spiritual ancestors refuse to feast until they had first fasted? And why did C.F.W. Walther, preaching during the Civil War, warn that Americans had grown too proud to repent?


In this episode, we explore:


The forgotten Christian practice of national days of repentance


How Thanksgiving originally followed fasting and public confession


Why prosperity is spiritually dangerous


Abraham Lincoln’s proclamation of a national fast


Walther’s warning that Americans wanted blessings without humility



This is not a nostalgic history lesson—it’s a prophetic call to rediscover gratitude rooted in repentance, blessings received as undeserved mercy, and thanksgiving that begins at the foot of the cross.

item.title: Theology in Song: What the Bible Says About Music
item.itunes:title: Theology in Song: What the Bible Says About Music
item.description:
In this episode we explore the profound relationship between music and the Word of God. From David driving demons from Saul with his harp, to prophets receiving revelation through song, to Jesus and His disciples singing before the cross, Scripture teaches that music is far more than entertainment — it is a spiritual weapon, a vessel of prophecy, and a preacher of the Gospel.


We also look at Martin Luther’s theology of music, discovering why he called music “the greatest treasure in the world after the Word of God.” For Luther, music doesn’t decorate theology — it carries it.


In this journey you will learn:


🎼 Why demons flee from music filled with God’s Word

🎺 How prophecy and revelation are intertwined with singing

📖 Why the largest book of the Bible is a songbook

🕊️ How music shapes belief, memory, and confession

⚔️ Why Luther called music a weapon against the devil


Let the Word of Christ dwell in you richly — in song.

item.title: The Kingdom That Crushes Every Kingdom
item.itunes:title: The Kingdom That Crushes Every Kingdom
item.description:
Text: Daniel 2:31–45


In this extended, Luther-style sermon, we examine Daniel’s interpretation of Nebuchadnezzar’s dream—a towering statue representing every human kingdom, and the stone “cut not by human hands” that strikes and destroys it.


We confront the false hopes of our age: trusting in politicians, nations, movements, or human power to save us. Scripture reminds us that empires crumble, ideologies rot, and wealth decays. Only Christ, the living Stone, strikes down all rival powers and establishes a Kingdom that will never be destroyed.


This message is a call to repent of worldly confidence, cling to Christ alone, and rejoice in the eternal Kingdom that fills the earth—not by human might, but by God’s grace.


> “The stone that struck the statue became a great mountain and filled the whole earth.” — Daniel 2:35

item.title: Behold, I Create New Heavens and a New Earth
item.itunes:title: Behold, I Create New Heavens and a New Earth
item.description:
Text: Isaiah 65:17–25 | Last Sunday of the Church Year


In this extended, Luther-style sermon on Isaiah 65:17–25, we confront the false gospels of our age and hear the biblical promise of a new creation given not through politics, progress, or human achievement but Christ alone. Just as justification is the work of God without our help, so the future world is not a human project but a divine gift. This message proclaims that God does not remodel the old creation or improve our sinful condition—He destroys the old and raises the new.


We expose the idolatry of political parties, activism, nationalism, utopian idealism, social engineering, and self-saving religion. Against these false hopes, Isaiah announces that God alone creates the new heavens and the new earth. In the resurrection to come, He will wipe away remembrance of sin, remove boasting, end war, and grant labor without pain—all through the crucified and risen Christ.


This is not a call to fix the world, but to wait in faith for the One who is coming to replace it.

Come, Lord Jesus.

item.title: The Last Sunday: Christ, Not Politics, Saves
item.itunes:title: The Last Sunday: Christ, Not Politics, Saves
item.description:
As the Church year ends, Scripture confronts us with the return of Christ, the Final Judgment, and the passing away of every earthly kingdom. In this extended message we hear how our Lord uses wars, disasters, moral collapse, and the decay of nations as sermons calling us to repentance.


Drawing on Scripture, the Lutheran Confessions, Luther, Walther, and Francis Pieper, this episode boldly exposes one of the greatest idols of our age: trusting politics and politicians for salvation. We face the errors of Christian nationalism, civil religion, therapeutic theology, escapist rapture teachings, and a Christianity that uses the poor instead of serving them.


Yet above all, this episode proclaims comfort: the Judge who returns is the same Jesus who was judged for us. For those who trust in Christ alone, the Last Day is not fear, but joy.


> “Heaven and earth will pass away, but My words will not pass away.” — Matthew 24:35




📖 Repent. Watch. Love. Christ alone endures.

item.title: Pieper, Walther, and the War Against Philosophy in Theology
item.itunes:title: Pieper, Walther, and the War Against Philosophy in Theology
item.description:
In this episode we explore why C. F. W. Walther and Francis Pieper fiercely opposed the use of philosophy in theology—and why the Church must recover their clarity today. Drawing from the Scriptures, the Lutheran Confessions, Luther’s own writings, and the dogmatic legacy of the Missouri Synod’s founders, we follow their central conviction:

The Gospel is not discovered through human wisdom, but revealed by God alone.


We examine how philosophy consistently distorts doctrine; how it turns the theologian into a “theologian of glory”; how it inevitably redefines sin, grace, and Christ Himself; and how Pieper and Walther offered a positive alternative—Scripture alone, revealed theology, and the wisdom of the cross.


If you want to understand why confessional Lutheranism stands where it stands, and why every generation must guard the pulpit from philosophical intrusion, this episode will equip, convict, and encourage you.

item.title: Where Are the Confessional Lutheran Churches?
item.itunes:title: Where Are the Confessional Lutheran Churches?
item.description:
Why do faithful Lutherans constantly have to ask, “Where can I find a confessional church near me?”

This episode digs deep into the uncomfortable but unavoidable truth:

If the synod were united in doctrine and practice as Scripture and the Lutheran Confessions require, the question would never need to be asked.


In this extended episode, we explore:


The Scriptural demand for doctrinal unity (Romans 16:17; 1 Cor. 1:10; Titus 1:9)


The Confessions’ insistence on rejecting all contrary doctrine


Luther’s uncompromising stance on purity of teaching


Walther’s warning that churches claiming to be Lutheran while rejecting Lutheran doctrine are more dangerous than Methodists or other heterodox churches


Pieper’s insistence that tolerating false doctrine destroys faith


The difference between being traditional or liturgical and being truly confessional


Why modern Lutherans feel like they’re searching for “safe churches”


How repentance and a return to the Book of Concord is the only path forward



This episode pulls no punches. It is direct, confessional, and rooted in Scripture and the Lutheran fathers.

If you’ve ever asked, “Where are the confessional Lutheran churches?”—this episode explains why the question exists and what must change for it to disappear.

item.title: The Sins of Sodom: A Warning to America
item.itunes:title: The Sins of Sodom: A Warning to America
item.description:
What were the real sins of Sodom? Scripture gives a fuller picture than the modern debates allow. In this episode, we explore the biblical testimony—from Genesis, Ezekiel, Isaiah, Jude, and the teachings of the Church—showing that Sodom’s wickedness was more than sexual perversion. At its core was the same root that corrupts every nation and every heart: the love of self, the hatred of the other, and the rejection of God.


We trace how America mirrors these sins today—not only in exporting pornography and LGBT ideology, but also in our national pride, injustice, and hostility toward the outsider. We examine the government’s biblical responsibilities toward sojourners, how American foreign policy creates the very outsiders we later despise, and why Christians should see themselves as pilgrims rather than cultural gatekeepers.


This episode calls the Church to repentance, compassion, and courage, reminding us that Christ rescues Sodom-like sinners and forms His people into a community of truth and love.


#Christianity #Lutheran #BibleTeaching #Sodom #LawAndGospel #Immigration #Holiness #Repentance #Pilgrims

item.title: Luther Against Philosophy
item.itunes:title: Luther Against Philosophy
item.description:
In this episode we walk through Martin Luther’s Heidelberg Disputation of 1518 as he delivers one of the most devastating critiques of philosophy in the history of the Church. Far from offering gentle corrections, Luther tears down the entire scholastic system built on Aristotle and calls philosophy the enemy of grace when it enters theology.


We explore:


Why Luther believed Aristotle had corrupted the Church


How philosophy created the theology of glory


Luther’s claim that human virtue and free will are deadly sins before God


The difference between a theologian of glory and a theologian of the cross


Why the cross destroys philosophical religion


Luther’s insistence that reason must be crucified and the sinner must despair of himself


The bold declaration: “The cross alone is our theology.”



If you’ve ever wondered why Luther rejected Aristotle, metaphysics, free will, and philosophical virtue in theology, this episode lays it out clearly and forcefully.


Listen now to discover why Luther believed the church must choose between philosophy and Christ crucified.


Soli Deo Gloria.

item.title: One Blood, One Lord: Racism, Nationalism, and the Holy Nation of God
item.itunes:title: One Blood, One Lord: Racism, Nationalism, and the Holy Nation of God
item.description:
This episode confronts the sins of racism, nationalism, and hatred of the outsider through the Scriptures, the Book of Concord, and the writings of Martin Luther and C.F.W. Walther.


We explore how the Bible teaches both the dignity of all peoples—“made from one blood”—and the reality that the Christian Church is a holy nation, not defined by ethnicity or earthly borders but by Baptism, faith, and the Gospel.


In this expanded episode we examine:

• God’s creation of all nations from one blood (Acts 17).

• Israel’s election as the line of Christ—not racial superiority (Deut. 7).

• The Church as the holy nation of 1 Peter 2:9.

• Luther’s commentary on the Tower of Babel and the sin of national pride.

• Luther’s warnings against despising foreigners and immigrants, since Christians are “pilgrims and strangers” on earth.

• God’s commands to love the sojourner and His judgments on nations that mistreat immigrants (Lev. 19, Ex. 22, Mal. 3).

• Walther’s rejection of racial pride and nationalism as idolatry.

• Why racism, nationalism, and hatred of immigrants are violations of the First, Fifth, and Eighth Commandments.


This is not politics. This is the Law and the Gospel, a call to repentance, and a confession of the one holy nation created by the blood of Christ.


#Lutheran #BookOfConcord #HolyNation #LawAndGospel #Racism #Nationalism #Immigration #Luther #Walther #ChristAlone #ChristianPodcast

item.title: 2nd Last Sunday 2 Peter 3:3-14 What Sort of People Ought You to Be?
item.itunes:title: 2nd Last Sunday 2 Peter 3:3-14 What Sort of People Ought You to Be?
item.description:
In this episode, we explore St. Peter’s powerful warning and comfort in 2 Peter 3:3–14 as the Church approaches the end of the Church Year. Scripture teaches that scoffers will arise, the world will mock Christ’s return, and everything we cling to will one day be dissolved in the fire of God’s final judgment. But for the Christian, this is not a message of fear—it is a message of hope, repentance, and holy expectation.


Why does the Lord delay His coming? What does it mean that we are “not destined for wrath”? And what kind of lives should Christians live as we await the new heavens and the new earth where righteousness dwells?


This sermon calls us to sober watchfulness, confident faith, and lives of holiness grounded in Christ’s mercy.


Scripture: 2 Peter 3:3–14

Theme: “What Sort of People Ought You to Be?”


#Lutheran #Sermon #EndTimes #SecondComing #2Peter #LawAndGospel #ChristianHope #Eschatology #BibleTeaching #PodcastPreaching

item.title: War: God’s Rod of Judgment and the Call to Repentance
item.itunes:title: War: God’s Rod of Judgment and the Call to Repentance
item.description:
War is not merely the product of politics or power — it is God’s greatest temporal punishments for unbelief. In this episode, we explore Luther and Walther’s sobering words on war as the rod of God’s wrath, the need for national and personal repentance before lifting the sword, and the Christian soldier’s duty to discern whether a war is just and defensive before fighting.


Drawing from Scripture and the Lutheran Confessions, this episode calls us to humility, repentance, and faith in Christ — the only refuge who makes wars cease to the ends of the earth.


📖 “He who goes to war without repentance goes not to fight men, but to fight God.” — Martin Luther


#LutheranPodcast #WarAndRepentance #MartinLuther #CFCWalther #BookOfConcord #ChristianDoctrine #JustWar #Repentance #Faith #LawAndGospel #LastChristianChurchOnEarth

item.title: O Death
item.itunes:title: O Death
item.description:
“O death, where is thy sting? O grave, where is thy victory?” (1 Corinthians 15:55)


In this episode, we confront the reality of death—and the greater reality of Christ’s victory over it. Through the Scriptures, the Lutheran Confessions, and the comforting words of Luther and Walther, we learn that for those who are in Christ, death has lost its power. It is no longer a curse but a sleep.


We’ll explore how faith in the crucified and risen Lord transforms our dying into victory, how the Confessions call death “a blessed rest,” and how Luther could call the grave “a little bed” in which we sleep until Christ awakens us.


> “For the Christian, death is not the end, but a sleep until the Lord comes and knocks on the little grave and says: ‘Doctor Martin, get up!’ Then I will arise in a moment and will be eternally happy with Him.”

— Martin Luther




#Lutheran #ODeath #Theology #Resurrection #Faith #ChristianHope #ChristianPodcast #BookOfConcord #CFCWalther #MartinLuther #DeathAndResurrection #ConfessionalLutheran

item.title: Infant Faith
item.itunes:title: Infant Faith
item.description:
Many Christians deny that infants can believe — but in doing so, they turn faith into a human work rather than a divine gift. In this episode of The Last Christian Church on Earth, we examine the Biblical and confessional proof that infants truly have faith.


From David’s trust at his mother’s breasts (Psalm 22), to John the Baptist leaping for joy in the womb (Luke 1), to Jesus’ own words that “these little ones believe in Me,” Scripture shows that faith is not a product of human reason, but the miraculous work of the Holy Spirit.


Drawing from the Augsburg Confession, Apology, Large Catechism, and Smalcald Articles, this episode shows that to deny infant faith is to deny grace itself — and to make faith into a work of man rather than the gift of God.


🎧 “The Faith of Infants: God’s Gift, Not Man’s Work” — because salvation is God’s work from beginning to end.


#Lutheran #InfantFaith #Baptism #JustificationByFaith #BookOfConcord #ReformationFaith #TheLastChristianChurchOnEarth

item.title: Murderers in the Church and State
item.itunes:title: Murderers in the Church and State
item.description:
God commands us to honor rulers — but forbids us to trust them.

Scripture and history show that even wicked kings and corrupt bishops are used by God to restrain evil and accomplish His purposes. As Augustine said, “Remove justice, and what are kingdoms but great robberies?” And Luther warned that “the big murderers hang the little ones, and this is called justice.”


In this episode of The Last Christian Church on Earth, we explore how God rules the world and the visible church through both good and evil men, and why the Christian’s duty is not to trust in princes or pastors, but in Christ alone. We also look at the biblical command to flee from false teachers, keeping the faith pure even when the church around us has fallen.


Key Topics:


The biblical basis for honoring rulers


Why God forbids us to trust in princes


Augustine on empires as “great robberies”


Luther on God using “big thieves” to punish lesser criminals


The same truth applied to corrupt church leaders


The command to flee false teachers (Matthew 7:15, Romans 16:17)


How Christ rules through His Word even when men fail



Featured Quotes:


> “Remove justice, and what are kingdoms but great robberies?” — Augustine

“The big murderers hang the little ones, and this is called justice.” — Luther

“Put not your trust in princes.” — Psalm 146:3




Christ alone is our King.

Honor authority — but flee from falsehood.


🎧 The Last Christian Church on Earth — standing firm on the Word of God in the last days.


Hashtags:

#LutheranPodcast #TheLastChristianChurchOnEarth #Augustine #Luther #FalseTeachers #HonorAuthority #SolaScriptura #BiblicalTruth #JustificationByFaith #Psalm146 #Romans13

item.title: When Shepherds Go Astray: The Need for Pastoral Discipline in the LCMS
item.itunes:title: When Shepherds Go Astray: The Need for Pastoral Discipline in the LCMS
item.description:
This episode calls the Lutheran Church—Missouri Synod back to the teaching of C.F.W. Walther’s Church and Ministry. We expose how the LCMS “reconciliation process” replaces repentance with bureaucracy and how true unity can only be found through the pure Word and faithful discipline. Includes readings from Walther’s Theses on Church and Ministry.


#LCMS #Walther #BookOfConcord #ChurchDiscipline #LutheranPodcast #ConfessionalLutheran #JustificationByFaith #LastChristianChurchOnEarth #ChristianPodcast

item.title: The Pope is the Antichrist and Justification By Faith Alone
item.itunes:title: The Pope is the Antichrist and Justification By Faith Alone
item.description:
Why do Lutherans confess that the Pope is the Antichrist? This episode explains the biblical and confessional basis for that teaching and shows, with Franz Pieper, that it all comes down to justification by faith alone.


Hashtags:

#Lutheran #Antichrist #JustificationByFaith #BookOfConcord #ChristAlone #SolaFide #ReformationFaith #TheLastChristianChurchOnEarth

item.title: Souwannabeatheologian
item.itunes:title: Souwannabeatheologian
item.description:
In this episode of The Last Christian Church on Earth Podcast, we explore Martin Luther’s profound teaching on how God forms a true theologian — not through human wisdom or academic study, but through prayer, meditation on Scripture, and suffering.


Luther’s threefold path — oratio, meditatio, tentatio — shows how theology is born in the crucible of trial and under the shadow of the cross. We’ll reflect on how meditation draws us into the lives and sufferings of the saints, how persecution often arises from within the visible church, and why the cross is the only true school of theology.


If you’ve ever wondered why the Christian life includes so much struggle, or how God uses trials to deepen faith, this episode will guide you to the comfort of the Gospel: that the deepest knowledge of God comes not through success, but through the cross of Christ.


Keywords:

Martin Luther, theology of the cross, Lutheran theology, Reformation, Christian suffering, spiritual growth, Scripture meditation, temptation, faith under trial, persecution, visible church, theologian, oratio meditatio tentatio, sanctification, discipleship, theology podcast, Christian teaching, Lutheran podcast, gospel-centered life


Hashtags:

#TheologyOfTheCross #MartinLuther #LutheranPodcast #ChristianFaith #ChristianPodcast #ReformationTruth #SpiritualWarfare #MeditateOnScripture #FaithUnderFire #TheCrossOfChrist #BiblicalTeaching #TheLastChristianChurchOnEarth

item.title: Trinity 21 — The Armor of God in the Evil Day
item.itunes:title: Trinity 21 — The Armor of God in the Evil Day
item.description:
In this sermon for Trinity 21, The Last Christian Church on Earth proclaims the Word of St. Paul from Ephesians 6:10–17: “Put on the whole armor of God.” The Christian life is a battle not against flesh and blood, but against the unseen powers that war against the soul. Many today mistake this struggle for politics, culture, or personal success—but the true warfare is spiritual.


This sermon calls believers to stand firm in Christ’s righteousness, armed with truth, faith, and the Word of God. It reminds the Church that victory is not found in earthly strength or strategy, but in the crucified and risen Lord who clothes His saints for battle in the evil day.

item.title: Trinity 20 — The Call to the Wedding Feast
item.itunes:title: Trinity 20 — The Call to the Wedding Feast
item.description:
In this sermon for Trinity 20, The Last Christian Church on Earth proclaims Christ’s invitation to the great wedding feast of the Lamb. The Gospel calls not only the outwardly religious but also the weary, the wandering, and the forgotten—to be clothed with the righteousness of Christ alone. Yet many reject the invitation, choosing the garments of their own works or the distractions of the world.

Here we confront the danger of indifference, the deceit of self-righteousness, and the mercy of God who still sends His servants into the highways and hedges to compel sinners to come in.


Rooted in Scripture and the Confessions, this sermon calls the hearer to repentance, faith, and holy living as those who have been found and clothed by grace.

item.title: Commemoraton of the Faithful Departed - 1 Thes 4:13–18
item.itunes:title: Commemoraton of the Faithful Departed - 1 Thes 4:13–18
item.description:
In this sermon for the Commemoration of the Faithful Departed, Pastor Charles Wiese of The Last Christian Church on Earth reflects on the hope and comfort we have in Christ amid death and loss. Drawing from Holy Scripture and the confessional Lutheran faith, this message reminds us that the Church on earth and the Church in heaven remain one in Christ—united by His cross, His Word, and His promise of the resurrection.


We remember the saints who have gone before us, not with despair, but with thanksgiving for their witness and with faith in the Lamb who has triumphed over death. This sermon calls all believers to live as pilgrims who look for the life of the world to come, trusting that those who die in the Lord are blessed and at rest from their labors.

item.title: All Saints’ Day Sermon Rev. 7:9-17
item.itunes:title: All Saints’ Day Sermon Rev. 7:9-17
item.description: This All Saints’ Day sermon reminds us that the Church is one in Christ — the saints who rest in Him and the faithful who still journey on earth. Rooted in Scripture and the Book of Concord, it proclaims our hope in the Lamb who conquered death and gathers His people from every nation. In Christ alone, we share the victory that no grave can take away.

item.title: A Reformation Sermon for Our Time-Galatians 5:1
item.itunes:title: A Reformation Sermon for Our Time-Galatians 5:1
item.description:
Reformation Today: Standing Faithfully in Our Time

This sermon reflects on the Reformation and its call to faithfulness in our world today. Grounded in Scripture and the Book of Concord, we examine how Christians are called to remain true to Christ, confess the Gospel boldly, and live as faithful strangers and pilgrims in these last days.

item.title: An Introduction
item.itunes:title: An Introduction
item.description: In this first episode, Pastor Charles Wiese introduces The Last Christian Church on Earth—a confessional Lutheran ministry calling believers back to the pure Gospel, repentance, and faithfulness to Christ’s Word. Learn the story behind the name and what to expect in future episodes of preaching, teaching, and encouragement for those who still hold fast to the truth in these last days.
